---
title: "Full-Text Index Optimisation"
subtitle: "Query Processing and Optimisation"
author: "Team 3"
date: "2026-03-24"
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
---

## Project overview

This project benchmarks MySQL `FULLTEXT` search against multi-column wildcard `LIKE` queries on text-rich Airbnb listing data.

## Why optimize text search?

A query such as:

```sql
SELECT *
FROM listings
WHERE description LIKE '%beach%';
```

must inspect rows sequentially because the leading wildcard prevents a conventional index lookup. A full-text index instead tokenizes text, removes configured stop words, and stores an inverted mapping from terms to matching rows.

```sql
CREATE FULLTEXT INDEX idx_fulltext
ON listings(description);

SELECT *
FROM listings
WHERE MATCH(description)
      AGAINST ('beach' IN NATURAL LANGUAGE MODE);
```

## Dataset and benchmark

- Source: Inside Airbnb.
- Raw dataset: 36,261 listings and 85 attributes.
- Search fields include listing names, descriptions, neighbourhood overviews, host descriptions, and amenities.
- Benchmark: 34 realistic Airbnb keywords and phrases.
- Repetitions: 10 runs per query to calculate average search time.
- Comparison: multi-column `%LIKE%` against multi-column `MATCH() AGAINST()`.

## Results

The main benchmark reports:

| Metric | FULLTEXT | Non-optimized LIKE |
|---|---:|---:|
| Average query time | 16.69 ms | 169.58 ms |
| Rows examined | approximately 1 | 33,407 |
| Average time reduction | **90.16%** | — |

Performance varied with query selectivity.

| Query | Matches | FULLTEXT | LIKE | Speedup |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| `highly recommend` | 29 | 0.31 ms | 215.67 ms | 700.23x |
| `restaurant` | 1,835 | 1.19 ms | 160.81 ms | 135.37x |
| `private` | 17,668 | 4.74 ms | 137.03 ms | 28.91x |
| `walking distance` | 4,677 | 25.01 ms | 166.88 ms | 6.67x |
| `washer dryer` | 2,265 | 69.21 ms | 207.07 ms | 2.99x |

## Complexity and trade-offs

The project characterizes wildcard scanning as approximately $O(N)$ and indexed retrieval as approximately $O(\log N + M)$, where $N$ is dataset size and $M$ is the number of matches.

Benefits:

- Faster retrieval and improved read scalability.
- Particularly strong gains for selective searches.
- Relevance scoring in natural-language mode.

Costs and limitations:

- Additional index storage.
- Slower inserts and updates.
- Text columns only; structured filters need other indexes.
- Search semantics differ from arbitrary substring matching.
- Stop-word and tokenization configuration affects recall.

## Recommendation

Full-text indexing is well suited to read-heavy, text-dense search systems. Before production adoption, benchmarks should verify semantic equivalence, cache conditions, relevance quality, database configuration, and performance at the intended scale.

## Source artifact

- [Full-text indexing presentation](ISSS625_Team3_Full%20Text%20Index.pptx)
